findings.title: [RESOLVED] Round-1 #1 ‚Äî rename return value ignored
findings.description: Round-1 finding #1 is resolved in bf10d40. `AppModule.provideSanctumDatabase` now branches via a `when` on `dbFile.renameTo(File(parent, corruptName))`: the success branch deletes the -journal/-wal/-shm sidecars and logs `"renamed to $corruptName"`; the refuse branch calls `dbFile.delete()` + sidecar deletes and logs `"rename refused ‚Äî corrupt file deleted"`; a pre-check branch handles the `parent == null || !dbFile.exists()` degenerate case with `"no-op (missing parent or file)"`. The error log line composition `"db open failed ‚Äî $renameOutcome"` now accurately reflects the post-condition on disk for every branch, so audit integrity and incident triage are no longer misled. Additionally, `Room.databaseBuilder.build()` is now folded inside the try block (with a nullable `firstAttempt`), so a builder-time exception also triggers the rename-and-retry path rather than escaping uncaught.
findings.impact: No further action required. The fix additionally removes a secondary crash path (fresh build opening on top of the still-corrupt main file when rename refuses), which was a pre-existing minor latent in round-1 code.
findings.recommendation: No action. See finding below on forensic-evidence side effect of the new delete fallback.

findings.title: [RESOLVED] Round-1 #4 ‚Äî @Volatile missing on AppCorruptionState.corruptionOccurred
findings.description: Round-1 finding #4 is resolved in bf10d40. `AppCorruptionState.corruptionOccurred` is now annotated `@Volatile` with an inline comment documenting the single-writer (Hilt injection thread in `provideSanctumDatabase`) / single-reader (Compose main thread in Task-10 `LaunchedEffect`) contract. Kotlin's `@Volatile` lowers to JVM `volatile` on the generated field, which provides the happens-before guarantee required for cross-thread Boolean visibility on ARM. For the current access pattern (set-once boolean, read-once per launch) this is sufficient ‚Äî no CAS or compound read-modify-write operations exist, so `AtomicBoolean` would be over-engineering.
findings.impact: No further action required. The one-time "–∏—Å—Ç–æ—Ä–∏—è –ø–æ–≤—Ä–µ–∂–¥–µ–Ω–∞" banner in Task 10 will now see a consistent view of the flag.
findings.recommendation: No action. If Task 10 subsequently wants reactive observation (e.g. corruption detected after the first composition), upgrade to `MutableStateFlow<Boolean>` at that point; not needed for the current acceptance criterion.

findings.title: [UNRESOLVED] Round-1 #2 ‚Äî StartupHousekeeper recursive delete still follows symlinks
findings.description: Round-1 finding #2 remains outstanding ‚Äî intentionally out of scope for this round. `StartupHousekeeper.deleter` (line 46-49) still calls `dir.deleteRecursively()` without symlink refusal, and `cleanupOrphanStagingDirs` (line 80-92) still enumerates `.staging-*` entries with `listFiles` and hands each one to the deleter. Threat model unchanged: `filesDir` is app-private (0700) on modern Android, so a symlink under `attachments/.staging-*` requires either (a) a separate path-traversal bug in attachment staging or (b) a rooted device. No exploitation path exists in the currently-merged code. Remains a defence-in-depth minor per MASVS MSTG-STORAGE / MOBILE-01.
findings.impact: Unchanged from round 1: no active attack path, defence-in-depth only. If a future Task introduces user-influenced filenames under `attachments/`, this becomes a prerequisite fix.
findings.recommendation: Unchanged from round 1. When this is addressed, use `Files.isSymbolicLink(path) || !Files.isDirectory(path, LinkOption.NOFOLLOW_LINKS)` to refuse symlink descent, or canonical-path containment check against `attachmentsDir.canonicalPath`. Tracking for a future hardening task is acceptable.

findings.category: A09: Security Logging and Monitoring Failures
findings.title: [UNRESOLVED] Round-1 #3 ‚Äî runBlocking on Hilt injection thread
findings.description: Round-1 finding #3 remains outstanding ‚Äî intentionally out of scope. `provideSanctumDatabase` still calls `runBlocking { errorLog.e(LOG_COMPONENT, "db open failed ‚Äî $renameOutcome", cause) }` at line 93-95. The call still pins the Hilt injection thread (typically main) for the duration of the `mutex.withLock` + `withContext(Dispatchers.IO)` file append inside `ErrorLog.e`. The log message interpolation `"db open failed ‚Äî $renameOutcome"` remains a PII-safe literal (the three branch strings are deterministic, `corruptName` is a timestamp derived from the device clock, and `cause` is bounded by ErrorLog's 200-char sanitiser and control-whitespace strip). No confidentiality regression; availability/ANR concern unchanged.
findings.impact: Unchanged from round 1: no confidentiality impact, availability-only. Remains preferable to document the synchronous-log trade-off (durability before CrashHandler can observe the process) rather than act on it.
findings.recommendation: Unchanged from round 1. Either keep `runBlocking` with an explanatory comment, or drain the log write via `SanctumApplication.appScope.launch(Dispatchers.IO)` through a volatile pending-log field on `AppCorruptionState`. No security remediation required.

findings.title: [UNRESOLVED ‚Äî with new side-effect] Round-1 #5 ‚Äî second-resolution corrupt timestamp, now amplified by delete-fallback branch
findings.description: Round-1 finding #5 remains outstanding ‚Äî `CORRUPT_TIMESTAMP_PATTERN = "yyyyMMdd-HHmmss"` (AppModule.kt:25) still has one-second granularity and is still derived from `LocalDateTime.now()` at system-default zone (not `Locale.ROOT` as the task spec phrases it ‚Äî `Locale.ROOT` only controls locale-dependent symbols in the formatter). NEW consideration introduced by bf10d40: the round-1 fix added a `dbFile.delete()` fallback when `renameTo` refuses (line 85). In that branch the corrupt main file is destroyed outright with no forensic copy retained, which amplifies the evidence-loss angle ‚Äî not just "the earlier snapshot is clobbered within the same second" but also "when rename refuses, no snapshot exists at all". The trade-off is deliberate per the commit message (fresh build must land on a clean slate to avoid an infinite-recovery-loop via CrashHandler), so this is NOT a regression; it is a documented reliability/forensics trade-off worth surfacing together with finding #5 for the Task-10 follow-up.
findings.impact: Forensic / incident-response only. Worst case (rare): a device with `renameTo` refusing (locked file, cross-device db path after a system-managed storage migration, adopted-storage quirks) loses all post-mortem evidence of the corruption event. An attacker cannot exploit this; support/observability only.
findings.recommendation: When this is picked up, do BOTH: (a) bump the timestamp pattern to `"yyyyMMdd-HHmmss-SSS"` + a short `UUID.randomUUID().toString().take(8)` suffix so two corruption events within the same second never collide; (b) in the rename-refused branch, attempt a best-effort `Files.copy(dbFile.toPath(), corruptPath, StandardCopyOption.REPLACE_EXISTING)` before `dbFile.delete()` so at least a copy of the corrupt file survives when rename cannot. Both are minor and can ride along with any later AppModule touch.
